home.title: Earthing resistance. From calculation to proof.
home.subtitle: Calculate grounding resistance, determine the optimal rod depth and automatically generate a NEN 1010 delivery report. One platform for installers who want to work faster and deliver with confidence.
home.card1Desc: Calculate the maximum grounding resistance per NEN 1010. Choose your grid, installation type and RCD — get four output layers instantly.
home.card2Desc: Enter a postal code. EarthGND automatically retrieves soil type, groundwater depth and pH from BRO — and calculates the required rod depth.
home.pillar1Desc: Soil type, groundwater depth and pH directly from the Dutch Soil Registration by postal code.
home.pillar2Desc: All calculations conform to NEN 1010, NEN 62305 and NEN 50522 — including legal maximum.
home.pillar3Desc: From rod depth calculation to a digitally signed NEN 1010 part 6 delivery report — including field measurement and file archive.

featuresPaid.f1: BRO soil data by postal code

workflow.step1Desc: Enter the postal code. BRO provides soil type, groundwater and pH. The calculator determines the required rod depth.
workflow.step2Desc: Send the field engineer a digital task order by email — including expected rod depth and target resistance.
workflow.step3Desc: The engineer confirms the field measurement on their phone. EarthGND archives the measured result.
workflow.step4Desc: Generate and sign the NEN 1010 part 6 delivery report. Immediately available in your project file.

meta.title: EarthGND — Professional Grounding Calculations
meta.description: Calculate grounding resistance and rod depth using BRO soil data. Compliant with NEN 1010, NEN 62305 and NEN 50522. Free Resistance Calculator, Pro Rod Depth Calculator.

faq.title: Frequently asked questions
faq.subtitle: Technical answers on grounding, NEN standards and the EarthGND calculators.
faq.q1: How do I calculate the maximum grounding resistance?
faq.a1: The maximum grounding resistance depends on the grid system (TT or TN), the type of RCD and the touch voltage limit (UL). The formula is Ra ≤ UL / Ia. The EarthGND Resistance Calculator computes this instantly per NEN 1010, NEN 62305 and NEN 50522.
faq.q2: How deep does a grounding rod need to be?
faq.a2: Required rod depth depends on soil resistivity (ρ), groundwater depth and target resistance. In dry sandy soil this may be 6–15 m; in clay or wet soil often 3–6 m. The Rod Depth Calculator computes this precisely using BRO soil data for your Dutch postal code.
faq.q3: What does NEN 1010 require for grounding resistance?
faq.a3: NEN 1010 states Ra ≤ UL / Ia. For a 30 mA RCD: Ra ≤ 1667 Ω. For a B16 circuit breaker without RCD: Ra ≤ 0.63 Ω. EarthGND calculates the exact limit for each installation scenario.
faq.q4: What is BRO soil data and how does it work?
faq.a4: BRO (Basis Registratie Ondergrond) is the Dutch national soil registration database. EarthGND connects to it automatically: enter a postal code and the calculator retrieves soil type, groundwater depth and pH from the nearest measurement point.
faq.q5: When are parallel grounding rods recommended?
faq.a5: Parallel rods are needed when a single rod cannot achieve the target resistance — typically for low Ra requirements (< 1 Ω) or unfavourable soil. EarthGND automatically advises on the number of rods and minimum spacing.
faq.q6: What is the difference between TT and TN grounding systems?
faq.a6: In a TT system the installation earthing is independent and relies on a local earth rod. In a TN system the supply neutral is already earthed. Maximum grounding resistance requirements differ significantly: TT systems with RCDs impose stricter Ra limits.
faq.q7: What do risk classes I–IV mean for grounding?
faq.a7: Risk classes (per NEN 62305 / EN 50522) indicate lightning protection requirements: class I is the highest risk (buildings with hazardous content), class IV the lowest. Soil resistivity affects the classification. EarthGND displays the risk class after each calculation.
faq.q8: Can EarthGND be used for grounding calculations in Germany or Belgium?
faq.a8: Dwight's formula and NEN/EN standards are internationally applicable. However, BRO soil data is only available for Dutch postal codes. For locations outside the Netherlands, you can manually enter soil resistivity based on a local soil survey.
